coredata.dc:title: Protein coronas formed by three blood proteins and food-borne carbon dots from roast mackerel: Effects on cytotoxicity and cellular metabolites
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Guoxin
Zhang, Lijuan
Zaky, Ahmed A.
Liu, Ronggang
Wang, Haitao
Abd El-Aty, A,M,
Tan, Mingqian
coredata.dc:description: Food-borne carbon dots (FCDs) produced naturally during food thermal processing are one of important factors affecting human health. The FCDs will inevitably encounter blood proteins after oral administration and spontaneously form protein coronas. In this study, the interaction of three major blood proteins, including albumin, gamma globulin, and fibrinogen, with FCDs from roasted mackerel was investigated for the first time. The purpose of the research is to explore the effect of the protein corona on the biological effects of cytotoxicity and the metabolic response. The results showed that FCDs spontaneously bound to the three blood proteins, and the process involved the participation of multiple interaction forces. Three protein coronas attenuated FCD-mediated cell viability damage, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial membrane potential. Further metabolomics analysis showed that FCDs disrupted cellular carbohydrate, amino acid, and nucleotide metabolism and significantly affected the expression of six metabolic pathways in normal rat kidney cells. The protein corona alleviated the disorder of energy and substance metabolism pathways. However, the protein corona inevitably expands the range of affected metabolic responses. The results of this study are of great value in exploring the toxicity characteristics of FCDs and their protein coronas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food-borne carbon dots
Protein corona
Interaction
Cytotoxicity
Cellular metabolism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interplay between FCDs and plasma proteins

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Foundation of Liaoning Province Education Administration

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Dalian Science and Technology Star Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31872915 ), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program ( XLYC1902120 ), Liaoning Province Education Administration ( J2020102 ), and Dalian Science and Technology Star Program ( 2019RQ067 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of FCDs interacting with three proteins to form protein coronas. Heat release (μJ/s) curves with time (s). (b) HSA-FCDs, (c) HGG-FCDs, (d) HF-FCDs. Fitting curves of the heat of reaction (kJ/mol) to molecular molar ratio. (e) HSA-FCDs, (f) HGG-FCDs, and (g) HF-FCDs.

The cytotoxicity of NRK cells. Cell viability cultured with FCDs (0.1 μg/mL-10 mg/mL) for (a) 24 h and (b) 48 h with serum-free DMEM as a control. The viability of cells cultured with (c) HSA, HGG, HF (0.001–0.1 mg/mL) and (d) protein coronas (10 μg/mL) for 48 h.
ROS levels in NRK cells treated with FCDs and protein coronas. (a) Fluorescence microscopy (green: DCFH-DA) with a scale of 60 μm. (b) Average fluorescence intensity of cells relative to the control.

MMP levels in NRK cells treated with FCDs and protein coronas. (a) Fluorescence microscopy (JC-1 fluorescent probe) with a scale of 60 μm. (b) Average fluorescence intensity of cells relative to the control.

Heatmap with normalization and cluster analysis of 58 cellular metabolites with significant changes in the FCDs and HSA-FCDs treatment groups compared with the control (P < 0.05). The relative expression levels of low and high metabolites are expressed in blue and red, respectively.
The results of pathway analysis of the metabolomic data based on the KEGG database. Compared with the control, the effects of (a) FCDs and (b) HSA-FCDs on metabolic pathways.

Analysis of carbohydrate metabolic pathways. Compared with the control, the effects of FCDs and HSA-FCDs treatments on metabolite levels in various metabolic pathways of cells were investigated. The arrow color between metabolites corresponds to the label color of the outer frame of the pathway. The level changes of each metabolite after FCDs and HSA-FCDs treatment are shown in the upper right corner.

Trend analysis of metabolite content in major pathways of carbohydrate metabolism, including the glycolysis pathway, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway, and glyoxylate and dicarboxylate metabolism. Expression level changes of (a) D-glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, (b) aconitate, (c) 2-keto-isovalerate, (d) succinate, (e) fumarate, (f) malate, (g) 6-phospho-D-gluconate, (h) ribose-phosphate, and (i) glyoxylate in cells treated with FCDs and HSA-FCDs compared with the control (P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Protein coronas formed by three blood proteins and food-borne carbon dots from roast mackerel: Effects on cytotoxicity and cellular metabolites

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Qinggongyuan 1, Ganjingzi District, Dalian 116034, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food-borne carbon dots (FCDs) produced naturally during food thermal processing are one of important factors affecting human health. The FCDs will inevitably encounter blood proteins after oral administration and spontaneously form protein coronas. In this study, the interaction of three major blood proteins, including albumin, gamma globulin, and fibrinogen, with FCDs from roasted mackerel was investigated for the first time. The purpose of the research is to explore the effect of the protein corona on the biological effects of cytotoxicity and the metabolic response. The results showed that FCDs spontaneously bound to the three blood proteins, and the process involved the participation of multiple interaction forces. Three protein coronas attenuated FCD-mediated cell viability damage, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial membrane potential. Further metabolomics analysis showed that FCDs disrupted cellular carbohydrate, amino acid, and nucleotide metabolism and significantly affected the expression of six metabolic pathways in normal rat kidney cells. The protein corona alleviated the disorder of energy and substance metabolism pathways. However, the protein corona inevitably expands the range of affected metabolic responses. The results of this study are of great value in exploring the toxicity characteristics of FCDs and their protein coronas.

ce:section.ce:para: Endogenous nanoparticles produced during food thermal processing are essential food safety suspects because of their uncertain bioeffects. Much more research is needed to evaluate their safety properties. In previous studies, food-borne carbon dots (FCDs) produced from food components could enter the heart, liver, lung, and brain through the bloodstream after oral administration [1,2]. During this process, the FCDs inevitably contact plasma proteins and spontaneously adsorb proteins in the blood and other body fluids to form protein coronas [3,4]. The formation process of the protein corona is complicated by the characteristics of nanoparticles and many factors in the biological system. The size, morphology, and surface chemistry of nanoparticles can affect the formation process and in vivo behavior of the protein corona [5]. At the same time, the complexity of the biological environment of proteins and the interaction between biomolecules endow the component variability of the protein corona [6]. The formation of the protein corona affects the surface physicochemical properties of nanoparticles, as well as the structure and function of proteins. The formed protein corona then affects the characteristics and functions, biocompatibility, in vivo circulation, and cell absorption of nanoparticles [7]. Assessing the biological effects of the protein corona and elucidating its biological significance are challenging tasks.

Previous work has reported the formation dynamics, composition evolution [8,9], and protein mapping [10] of protein coronas of nanoparticles incubated with total plasma proteins. However, the protein composition and factors affecting the formation of protein coronas are so complicated that it is a challenge to demonstrate the formation and biological effects of protein coronas. Although some studies have examined the interaction of nanoparticles with human serum albumin (HSA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA), the most prevalent protein in plasma [11–13], it is insufficient to reveal the potential significance of protein coronas and their biological influences in detail. There is still a lack of research on the interaction and biological evaluation of the protein corona formed with FCDs.

The cytotoxicity of nanoparticles represents their adverse effect on vital human tissues [14]. It is necessary to explore the cytotoxicity of FCDs after forming protein coronas. Due to the variability and complexity of nanoparticle characteristics and physiological environments, the cytotoxicity of nanoparticles is quite different [15]. The FCDs have been confirmed to have potentially adverse effects on living cells, such as decreasing cell viability, inducing oxidative stress, and causing mitochondrial dysfunction [16,17]. The cytotoxicity of FCDs is highly related to the processing temperatures of food items [18] and is also positively correlated with the concentration of FCDs [19,20]. The protein corona may interact with the functional groups on nanoparticle surfaces, affect redox activity, and change nanoparticle cytotoxicity. For example, porous silica nanoparticles can induce the hemolysis of red blood cells, and the stable protein corona formed by the adsorption of plasma protein on the surface of nanoparticles can shield the original microenvironment of nanoparticles, thus reducing or even eliminating the hemolytic impact [21]. The protein corona formed by HSA and FCDs in roasted beef alleviated the swelling of the endoplasmic reticulum caused by FCDs, which inhibited the abnormal increase in reactive oxygen species (ROS) and decrease in mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) [22]. The contact between the surface of nanoparticles and the cell may affect the integrity of the cell membrane, cause irreversible physical damage, affect physiological barrier function, and even lead to cell death [23]. Investigations have shown that nanoparticles may cause metabolic disturbances in vital biological processes, interfering with the metabolism of carbohydrates, amino acids, and lipids [24,25]. However, the influence of FCDs and protein coronas on cell metabolism remains to be elucidated. To better understand the biological functions of protein coronas on FCDs and evaluate the safety of FCDs, it is crucial to explore the effects of FCDs and their protein coronas on cytotoxicity and cell metabolism.

In previous study, three blood-rich protein macromolecules, including HSA, γ-globulin (HGG) and fibrinogen (HF), were selected as model proteins to explore the possible interaction mechanism between FCDs and three proteins [26]. Herein, we focus on the biological effects of FCDs and three proteins before and after the formation of protein coronas in cellular metabolic level to gain more insights on organisms. First, the interaction between the three plasma proteins and FCDs extracted from roast mackerel was assessed through thermodynamic analysis. Then, the impacts of FCDs on cytotoxicity, including cell viability, ROS, and MMP levels, were investigated before and after the formation of protein coronas. Furthermore, the impacts of FCDs and HSA protein coronas on cellular metabolic pathways and the expression of characteristic metabolites were evaluated. The findings of this study can be used as a valuable reference for assessing the in vivo circulation safety of FCDs.

Fresh mackerel was procured from a local market in Ganjingzi District, Dalian, China. HSA (96–99 %) was acquired from Solarbio Technology (Beijing, China), and HGG (99 %) and HF (50–70 % protein, >80 % of protein is clottable) were secured from Sigma Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Superior grade fetal bovine serum was obtained from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai, China). High glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), 0.25 % Trypsin-EDTA (1×), and 1 × PBS (pH = 7.2–7.4) (cell-culture grade) were acquired from Solarbio Technology (Beijing, China). Penicillin–streptomycin solution was acquired from Biological Industries (Beijing, China). Reactive oxygen species (DCFH-DA) and mitochondrial membrane potential (JC-1) detection kits were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), chlorpromazine (CPZ), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD), and amiloride hydrochloride (ALHC) were supplied by Macklin (Shanghai, China). All other reagents used were chromatographic/analytical pure.

The extraction method of FCDs was based on our previous method [26]. Fresh mackerel meat (2000 g, wet weight) was cut into 1 × 1 × 2 cm pieces and roasted at 230 °C for 30 min. The obtained grilled fish meat (533 g) was added to ethanol at a ratio of 1:5 (w/v). Ultrasonic mixing was performed for 4 h, and the impurities were removed by suction filtration. The solution was dried using a rotary vacuum evaporator, followed by adding 50 mL of deionized water. Dichloromethane was used to extract the aqueous solution three times to remove the hydrophobic components. The aqueous phase was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 4 °C, 10 min) to remove the sediment, and the resulted supernatant was purified by a D101 macroporous adsorption resin column with deionized water as the eluent. The collected fluorescent fractions were evaporated, concentrated to 10–20 mL, and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solution was then purified by a semipreparative high-performance liquid chromatographic column C18 (× 300 mm, 10 μm), 10 % methanol aqueous solution was used as the mobile phase with a flow velocity of 18 mL/min. The fluorescence component was collected at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The collected solution was concentrated and freeze-dried to obtain FCDs powder (128 mg).

Isothermal titration calorimetry (Affinity ITC, TA Instruments, DE, USA) was used to determine the titration curve. The FCDs solution dissolved in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was titrated to 10 μmol/L protein solution (pH = 7.4, T = 298 K). The injection volume was 2 μL 40 times at a stirring rate of 80 rpm. Titration of FCDs into PBS was used as a blank control to exclude the dilution thermal effect. Independent binding models were selected to obtain thermodynamic parameters using NanoAnalyze software provided by TA Instruments, including enthalpy (ΔH), free energy (ΔG), and entropy (ΔS).

Normal rat kidney (NRK) cell lines were donated by Li Yu Laboratory of Tsinghua University (Beijing, China) and were cultured in DMEM (10 % FBS, 1 % penicillin/streptomycin, 5 % CO2, 37 °C) before each experiment.

Cytotoxicity was assessed by MTT assay. NRK cells were inoculated into 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. Serum-free DMEM was used to dissolve FCDs and proteins and cultured for 24 and 48 h, respectively. The concentration of FCDs ranged from 0.1 μg/mL to 10 mg/mL. The protein concentrations ranged from 0.001 to 1 mg/mL. The protein coronas were obtained by pre-incubating FCDs and proteins at 10 μg/mL. The cells were washed 3 times with PBS, MTT solution (5 mg/mL, 10 μL per well) was added and incubated in the incubator in the dark for 4 h, the supernatant was abandoned, and 200 μL dimethyl sulphone was added and shaken for 15 min. The absorbance at 570 nm was obtained using a microplate reader (Infinite M200, Tecan, Switzerland). The cell survival rate was calculated according to A570sample/A570control × 100 %.

NRK cells were inoculated into 12-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. Serum-free DMEM was used to dissolve FCDs and protein samples (all 10 μg/mL) and cultured for 24 h. After washing 3 times with PBS, 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) solution (1 mL, 10 μM per well) was added to each well, cultured in the dark for 30 min and washed 3 times with PBS to remove free DCFH-DA. Qualitative and quantitative tests were carried out. For qualitative experiments, cell images were recorded using a fluorescence microscope (Echo Labs, Broomfield, USA). For quantitative experiments, cells were treated with trypsin and resuspended in serum-free DMEM. The cell suspension was added to a 96-well plate. Fluorescence intensity was measured by a microplate reader (Infinite F200 PRO, Tecan, Switzerland) (EX = 485 nm, EM = 535 nm). The results were normalized to the control.

NRK cells were inoculated into 12-well plates (1 × 105 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. Serum-free DMEM was used to dissolve FCDs and protein samples (all 10 μg/mL) and cultured for 24 h. After washing 3 times with PBS, JC-1 fluorescent dye was added and cultured in the dark for 30 min. After washing 3 times with PBS, qualitative and quantitative testing were the same as in 2.5. The fluorescence intensity of J-monomers (EX = 485 nm, EM = 535 nm) and J-aggregates (EX = 535 nm, EM = 595 nm) was recorded, and the results were expressed as FLEX=535/FLEX=485.

NRK cells were inoculated into 6-well plates (1 × 106 cells per well) and cultured for 24 h. Serum-free DMEM was used to dissolve FCDs and HSA-FCDs samples (1 mg/mL) and cultured for 24 h. After washing 3 times with PBS, the medium was replaced and cultured for 2 h. The cell plates were placed on dry ice, and 80 % methanol, pre-cooled to −80 °C, was added and kept at −80 °C for 20 min. Undecanoic acid, 19-decanoic acid, and L-phenylalanine were used as internal standards (5 μg/mL). After collecting the solution, the cells were lysed by a homogenizer and centrifuged for 20 min (4 °C, 14000 g). The supernatant was dried by centrifugal concentration and stored at −80 °C. Before testing, every sample was redissolved with 200 μL acetonitrile and mixed in equal parts to prepare quality control samples. The samples were filtered by a 0.22 μm filter and analyzed by ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry. Chromatographic separation conditions and mass spectrometry parameters were conducted as previously described [27].

All results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) (n = 3). SPSS 19.0 software (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used to analyze one-way ANOVA, and Tukey's test was used to assess the statistical significance between groups. Statistically significant differences are marked as *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, and ***P < 0.001. SIMCA 14.1 (Umetrics, Umeå, Sweden) was used to complete multivariable statistical analysis, the heatmap was obtained using TBtools 6.2 software, and metabolic pathways were analyzed using MetaboAnalyst 5.0 (https://www.metaboanalyst.ca). Data were plotted using Origin 2018 (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interplay between FCDs and plasma proteins
ce:section.ce:para: The interactive features of FCDs with HSA, HGG, and HF were reported in our previous work [26]. Our data showed that the FCDs could rapidly bind with the three blood proteins to form non-luminescent ground state complexes within 30 s through the static mechanism (Fig. 1a). The FCDs binding ability was positively correlated with the molecular weight of the proteins, and the FCDs could affect the morphology, size, height, surface electrochemical properties, structure, and amino acid microenvironment of three proteins to varying degrees [26]. Herein, the interaction between FCDs and HSA, HGG, and HF was investigated in detail by isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) technology. Real-time heat flow and cumulative heat release during the reaction of samples were measured by the ITC method. The thermodynamic parameters of molecular interaction, interaction force, and binding stoichiometric ratio among molecules were obtained to evaluate the protein macromolecules and FCDs. Fig. 1b-d show the titration curves of HSA, HGG, and HF by FCDs. An independent site binding model was used to determine thermodynamic parameters (Fig. 1e-g). At 298 K, the binding constants (Ka) of FCDs with HSA, HGG, and HF increased successively to 0.80, 2.33, and 10.17 × 104 M−1, respectively, indicating that the affinity of HSA, HGG, and HF for FCDs gradually increased due to the differences in protein characteristics. The stoichiometric ratios of HSA, HGG, and HF to FCDs were 11.98, 11.16, and 25.30, respectively. Previous studies demonstrated that FCDs could lead to the aggregation of HGG [26], which may obscure the binding sites on the surface of HGG, so understandably, the combined stoichiometric ratio of HGG and FCDs did not significantly increase compared with HSA, which is not in conflict with the affinity trend. The negative values of ΔH and ΔG indicated spontaneous exothermic reactions between FCDs and the three proteins. In addition, ΔH < 0 and ΔS > 0 can usually prove the existence of electrostatic interaction forces [28,29], indicating the existence of electrostatic interactions between HSA and FCDs. Unlike the HSA system, the HGG and HF systems have relatively small negative enthalpy changes. Therefore, the binding reaction between HGG, HF, and FCDs can be attributed to the interaction of the hydrogen bond or van der Waals force [30]. The results of the thermodynamic analysis provided molecular information on the interaction between FCDs and proteins, laying a foundation for further exploration of the in vitro biological effects of protein coronas.

The cytotoxicity of FCDs before and after forming protein coronas showed that, when the concentration of FCDs was lower than 10 μg/mL, the cell viability was maintained at > 90 % compared with that of the control after incubation for 24 h (Fig. 2a). When the concentration of FCDs was higher than 10 μg/mL, the cell viability was significantly inhibited (P < 0.001). When the concentration of FCDs reached 10 mg/mL, the cell viability decreased to 44.8 ± 3.9 % (Fig. 2a). After culturing for 48 h, all concentrations of FCDs higher than 0.1 μg/mL significantly inhibited cell viability (< 80 %, P < 0.001), and cell viability decreased with increasing concentrations of FCDs, indicating that FCDs had dose-dependent toxicity to cells (Fig. 2b). The effect of individual proteins was also evaluated before assessing the cytotoxicity of the protein coronas. The results revealed that none of the three types of proteins affected cell viability after culturing for 24 or 48 h (Fig. 2c), which ruled out the possible protein-only effect for further evaluation of the impact of the protein corona. After forming the protein corona, all three proteins effectively alleviated the cytotoxicity caused by FCDs (Fig. 2d). Significance analysis showed that the remission degree of cell viability was HSA-FCDs > HGG-FCDs > HF-FCDs, contrary to the molecular weight of proteins and the affinity between proteins and FCDs in thermal analysis. These results indicated that the influence of protein coronas was related to the types and inherent properties of proteins, and protein coronas containing different components had different effects on cytotoxicity. This was probably due to the different binding affinity of HSA, HGG, and HF, thus causing other alleviated toxicity of FCDs after forming protein coronas.

The influence of protein coronas formed with HSA, HGG, and HF onto the FCDs on ROS clearance was then evaluated. Oxidative stress occurs when ROS levels significantly exceed the antioxidant capacity of biological systems, resulting in oxidative modifications of intracellular biomolecules and changes in their functions and behaviors and even leading to cell death [31,32]. Oxidative stress mediated by abnormally elevated levels of ROS is one of the common cytotoxic effects of nanoparticles [33]. After entering cells, 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) without fluorescence can be hydrolyzed by lactase to dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCFH), which oxidizes ROS to produce fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Therefore, the ROS level in cells is proportional to the fluorescence intensity [34]. Herein, ROS production in cells was measured to further evaluate the effect of FCDs and protein coronas on cytotoxicity. Fig. 3 shows that the FCDs increased ROS levels by 126.3 ± 4.8 %, demonstrating that FCDs might induce oxidative stress in cells, consistent with earlier research results on carbon nanoparticles [16,35]. After incubation with HSA, HGG, and HF and forming protein coronas, the ROS levels decreased to 88.4 %, 94.1 %, and 109.2 %, respectively, suggesting that the three protein coronas alleviated oxidative stress induced by FCDs. The effect was successively weakened, compatible with the remission trend of cytotoxicity. Plasma proteins have been reported to reduce the ROS induced by gold nanoparticles [36]. HSA alleviated ROS production in cells treated with FCDs from roast beef [22]. This finding is in line with the results of this experiment, implying that the formation of protein coronas relieves oxidative stress induced by FCDs and restores cell homeostasis. The degree of the effect depends on the protein type in the protein coronas.

Oxidative stress can lead to mitochondrial dysfunction and decrease MMP, an early apoptotic event. The analysis of MMP levels can provide further evidence for the nanoparticle impact of FCDs and their protein coronas on living cells [37]. As illustrated in Fig. 4a, J-aggregates can show red fluorescence, indicating a higher MMP, while JC-1 monomers show green fluorescence, denoting a lower MMP. The decrease in MMP can be easily detected by the fluorescence transition from red to green [38]. Quantitative analysis of fluorescence intensity showed that FCDs could significantly reduce MMP to 64.8 % compared with the control (Fig. 4b) (P < 0.001), suggesting that FCDs lowered MMP and promoted cell apoptosis. After incubation with HSA, HGG, and HF and form protein coronas, the level of MMP increased to 110.2 %, 102.2 %, and 96.6 %, respectively, implying that the three protein coronas had different alleviating effects on FCD-induced MMP decline and mitochondrial abnormality. This finding was in agreement with the trend of the ROS results. Notably, HSA-FCDs displayed significantly higher MMP than HSA alone (P < 0.001), as did HGG-FCDs (P < 0.01). One of the core functions of mitochondria is to maintain oxidative phosphorylation through the transmembrane potential gradient generated by the electron transport chain to drive ATP synthesis [39]. This result indicates that the formation of these two protein coronas may play a positive role in maintaining mitochondrial function.

To further explore the reasons for the effects of FCDs and protein coronas on cytotoxicity through the molecular mechanism and ensure the representativeness of samples and the operation of the same batch, a targeted metabolomics method was adopted to explore the impacts of FCDs and HSA-FCDs on cell metabolism. All samples were mixed to prepare quality control (QC) samples, which were analyzed after every 6 samples to guarantee the reliability and repeatability of the metabolic analysis. Principal component analysis (PCA) was used to analyze the metabolites detected in all samples. The PCA score diagram showed a clustering distribution of data in each group without outliers. All QC samples were closely distributed (Fig. S1a), indicating that the analysis method had good reproducibility and met quantitative analysis demands. The model reliability (R2X) was 0.648, and the model predictive power (Q2) was 0.535, denoting that the analysis model was reliable. Partial least squares discrimination analysis (PLS-DA) was further carried out. The FCD- and HSA-FCD-treated groups and the control group were distributed in three non-overlapping clusters, suggesting significant differences in cell metabolites after treatment (Fig. S1b). Furthermore, the R2Y and Q2 of the model were 0.886 and 0.784, respectively, proving the robustness of the established PLS-DA mathematical model. Two hundred random replacement tests were conducted to verify the PLS-DA model. In the verification results, the vector values of R2 and Q2 were lower than the original values (Fig. S1c), specifying that the model had not been overfitted [40]. In addition, the PLS-DA model loading diagram showed the differences in metabolites among each group of samples (Fig. S1d).

Further analysis of characteristic metabolites was conducted to explore the influence of FCDs and HSA-FCDs on the expression level changes of cell metabolites. Z score normalization and one-way ANOVA were used to analyze the 290 metabolites detected. Compared with the control, a total of 58 metabolites were significantly different in expression level (P < 0.05), among which 51 metabolites had significant differences after FCDs treatment, 49 metabolites were significantly different after HSA-FCDs treatment, and 12 metabolites were identified as metabolites with significant changes between the FCDs and HSA-FCDs treatment groups. Compared with the control, 12 metabolites were down-regulated, and 46 were up-regulated (Fig. 5 ). After FCDs and HSA-FCDs treatment, down-regulated metabolites are involved in carbohydrate metabolic pathways, such as nicotinamide, aspartate, NADPH, citrulline, and amino acid metabolites, such as pyridoxine, tyrosine, proline, and choline. The up-regulated metabolites are involved in various metabolic pathways, such as glycolysis, tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA cycle), pentose phosphate pathway, amino acid, and purine nucleotide metabolism, which reflects the effect of FCDs and HSA-FCDs on carbohydrate, amino acid, and nucleotide metabolism.

Next, the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) database was used to analyze the effects of FCDs and HSA-FCDs on various cellular metabolic pathways [41]. The impacts of various metabolic pathways between the treatment groups and the control can be intuitively observed, and a total of 42 affected metabolic pathways were identified. The range of -log(p) >4 and pathway impact value >0.15 were considered significantly affected pathways for further analysis [42] (Fig. 6 ). Compared with the control, FCDs treatment significantly affected 6 pathways, including glycine, serine and threonine metabolism, nicotinate and nicotinamide metabolism, phenylalanine, tyrosine and tryptophan biosynthesis, phenylalanine metabolism, TCA cycle, ubiquinone and other terpenoid-quinone biosynthesis. After HSA-FCDs treatment, the affected pathways were increased to 9, including the above 6 pathways. The increased pathways were the pentose phosphate pathway, riboflavin metabolism, glyoxylate and dicarboxylate metabolism, indicating that the protein corona formation made the metabolic pathway more complicated. According to the changes in metabolite expression levels in the pathways, treatment with FCDs and HSA-FCDs disturbed the TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway, amino acid metabolism, nucleotide metabolism and related pathways.

Fig. 7 illustrates the correlation between significantly affected metabolic pathways and the specific effects on the expression of the metabolites. Most metabolites in various metabolic pathways related to carbohydrate metabolism were significantly affected by FCDs and HSA-FCDs treatment (blue shading metabolites). A few metabolites, such as 6-phospho-gluconate and betaine, significantly changed only after FCDs treatment (green shading metabolites). Individual metabolites, such as serine (yellow shading metabolite), significantly changed only after HSA-FCDs treatment. These results designate that FCDs can disrupt carbohydrate metabolic pathways, and HSA-FCDs can partially recover or alleviate the effects of FCDs. However, treatment with HSA-FCDs can cause additional disturbance of a few metabolites due to the introduction of more substances. Comprehensive analysis of these metabolic pathways can provide a macroscopic understanding of the effects of FCDs and HSA-FCDs on various metabolic pathways.

Next, the metabolite expression levels in each pathway were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 8 , metabolite levels were significantly affected by the treatment groups in the main metabolic pathways involved in carbohydrate metabolism. After FCDs treatment, metabolite levels in the glycolysis pathway, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway, glyoxylate, and dicarboxylate metabolism were generally increased. D-glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate, aconitate, 2-keto-isovalerate, succinate, fumarate, malate, 6-phospho-D-gluconate, ribose-phosphate, and glyoxylate were selected as representatives. These results recommend that FCDs promote intracellular carbohydrate metabolism, the primary fuel for anaerobic and aerobic metabolism [43]. Therefore, activating these metabolic pathways can maintain the rate of ATP synthesis. This phenomenon corresponds to the fact that FCDs can increase intracellular ROS and decrease MMP because cells need more energy to respond to cellular stress. After HSA-FCDs treatment, fumarate and 6-phospho-D-gluconate returned to the control levels (Fig. 8e, g). The expression levels of most of the other metabolites involved in the TCA cycle and pentose phosphate pathway were significantly higher than those of the control but decreased compared with the FCDs treatment group (P < 0.05), indicating that HSA-FCDs had a repairing effect on cell carbohydrate metabolism disorder. This effect also corresponds to the alleviating effect of HSA-FCDs on cytotoxicity indexes.

After treatment with FCDs and HSA-FCDs, metabolite levels of glycine, serine, and threonine related to carbohydrate metabolic pathways were significantly affected (P < 0.05), as displayed in Fig. S2. After treatment with FCDs, the levels of the 5 metabolites guanidinoacetic acid, betaine, sarcosine, glyoxylate, and 2-oxobutanoate were significantly increased compared with the control (P < 0.05) (Fig. S2a, c, d, e, h). The levels of 2 metabolites, choline and methylcysteine, were significantly decreased (P < 0.05) (Fig. S2b, f). Other metabolites were not significantly affected, and the metabolites showed an overall upregulated trend. The catabolism of glycine, serine, and threonine is closely related to the flux of the TCA cycle. The increase in the TCA cycle induces the production of NADH, which further increases the membrane potential. When the membrane potential is high enough, proton/electron transport across the membrane drives ATP synthesis [44]. Therefore, the upregulation of glycine, serine, and threonine metabolism may explain the increased TCA cycle and enhanced energy metabolism. HSA-FCDs treatment significantly reduced the betaine levels induced by FCDs (P < 0.05) (Fig. S2c). Furthermore, it increased sarcosine and serine induced by FCDs (Fig. S2g), suggesting that HSA-FCDs treatment showed no apparent trend in regulating glycine, serine, and threonine metabolism.

After treatment with FCDs and HSA-FCDs, changes in the metabolite levels of other pathways with significant changes related to carbohydrate metabolic pathways are illustrated in Fig. S3, including phenylalanine, tyrosine and tryptophan biosynthesis, arginine biosynthesis, nicotinate and nicotinamide metabolism. After FCDs treatment, the levels of 4 metabolites, phenylpyruvate, hydroxyphenylpyruvate, quinolinate, and L-arginino-succinate, were significantly increased compared with the control (P < 0.05) (Fig. S3a, c, e, h). The levels of 4 metabolites, namely, tyrosine, aspartate, nicotinamide, and citrulline, decreased significantly (P < 0.05) (Fig. S3b, d, f, g). After HSA-FCDs treatment, the level of hydroxyphenylpyruvate returned to the control level, and the levels of other metabolites were still similar to those of the FCDs treatment group. Overall, FCDs can reshape metabolic pathways related to carbohydrate metabolism, and HSA-FCDs can mitigate the effects of FCDs on some metabolites, which corresponds to the effects of FCDs and HSA-FCDs on cytotoxicity.

In summary, FCDs spontaneously combined with three major blood proteins, HSA, HGG, and HF, through exothermic reactions. The formation of protein coronas involved electrostatic interactions, hydrogen bonding or van der Waals forces. The binding affinity of HSA, HGG, and HF to FCDs gradually increased, and the binding constants (Ka) were 0.80, 2.33, and 10.17 × 104 M−1, respectively. FCDs could lead to time- and dose-dependent cellular viability damage, and concentrations higher than 10 μg/mL could significantly inhibit cell viability. The FCDs could significantly increase the intracellular ROS level to 126.3 % of the control group and reduce the MMP level to 64.8 %, thus disrupting intracellular homeostasis. The formation of HSA, HGG, and HF protein coronas alleviated the above cytotoxicity induced by FCDs, and the degree of remission was HSA > HGG > HF. The FCDs significantly affected the expression of 6 cellular metabolic pathways and induced significantly abnormal expression levels of 58 metabolites. The formation of protein coronas contributed to the relaxation of FCD-induced disturbances in cellular carbohydrate, amino acid, and nucleotide metabolism. Notably, the superposition effect of protein corona components led to a more complex cellular metabolic pathway response. The species and intrinsic properties of proteins are important parameters that affect the binding ratio, affinity, and cellular physiological response of protein coronas. The results are of great significance for the safety evaluation of the biological effects of FCDs and their protein coronas.

In the future, it is crucial to explore the interaction between different protein components during the formation of protein coronas with FCDs to clarify the composition changes of protein corona during in vivo transport process. More advanced research techniques need to be developed to realize real-time identification of the dynamic evolution of protein corona components in physiological environment. The immune recognition of FCD protein coronas cannot be ignored. Measuring the expression of cytokines and immune-related proteins is helpful in exploring the influence on immune signaling pathways to comprehensively evaluate the biological behavior and effect of FCDs and their protein coronas.

Guoxin Cui: Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Formal analysis. Lijuan Zhang: Methodology. Ahmed A. Zaky: Writing - Review & Editing. Ronggang Liu: Methodology. Haitao Wang: Methodology. A. M. ABD EL-ATY: Formal analysis, Writing - Review & Editing. Mingqian Tan: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - Review & Editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31872915), Liaoning Revitalization Talents Program (XLYC1902120), Liaoning Province Education Administration (J2020102), and Dalian Science and Technology Star Program (2019RQ067).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cell metabolomics analysis, trend analysis of metabolite contents in major amino acid and other metabolic pathways related to carbohydrate metabolism are available in supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The supporting information of multivariable statistical analysis and metabolite content analysis in major amino acid and other metabolic pathways related to carbohydrate metabolism is available. Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2022.07.196.
